quick_facts.label: What it is
quick_facts.value: B.Sc. in Fashion Design is a provider-specific undergraduate programme combining fashion and apparel design with textile, garment-production and portfolio skills; there is not one single nationally prescribed B.Sc. syllabus or admission route.

quick_facts.label: Typical entry qualification
quick_facts.value: Class 12/10+2 is the usual entry qualification. Published institutional rules differ: CSMU states 10+2 eligibility, while VIT requires a pass in any stream with a 60% overall aggregate (or a relevant 10+3 diploma with 60%).

quick_facts.label: Typical duration
quick_facts.value: A conventional B.Sc. Fashion Design route is three academic years across six semesters; some institutions also advertise four-year honours or honours-with-research variants under their undergraduate frameworks.

quick_facts.label: NIFT exam relationship
quick_facts.value: The NIFT entrance route is for NIFT’s bachelor programmes, including B.Des. Fashion Design; it is not a universal entry requirement for B.Sc. Fashion Design offered by other universities. For example, VIT publishes merit- and interview-based admission with no entrance examination for its B.Sc. Fashion Design.

quick_facts.label: Fee context
quick_facts.value: Fees are institution- and session-specific. As published by VIT for its 2024–25 B.Sc. Fashion Design admission information, tuition was ₹90,000 plus a refundable ₹2,000 caution deposit; applicants should obtain the current offer letter/fee notice before applying.

sections.heading: Eligibility: normally Class 12, but thresholds vary by university
paragraphs: B.Sc. Fashion Design does not have a single India-wide eligibility rule. A common baseline is completion of 10+2 from a recognised board in any stream. CSMU’s B.Sc. Fashion Design ordinance states that candidates qualified in the 10+2 examination may apply.

Institutions can impose their own minimum marks, stream, diploma and category-relaxation conditions. For example, VIT’s published rule for B.Sc. Fashion Design is a pass in any stream with at least 60% overall aggregate, or a 10+3 diploma in Fashion, Textile, Apparel or Garment with at least 60%. Applicants should use the eligibility notice for the exact campus and admission year rather than assuming a common cutoff.
bullets: Usual starting point: recognised 10+2/Class 12 qualification.
Possible additional conditions: minimum aggregate, accepted-board equivalence, relevant diploma pathway, category-specific relaxation and document verification.
A science stream is not universally required; published examples from CSMU and VIT allow entry from any stream, subject to their stated conditions.

sections.heading: Duration and award structure
paragraphs: The conventional format is a three-year undergraduate degree comprising six semesters. CSMU’s ordinance explicitly describes B.Sc. Fashion Designing as three academic years over six semesters, and VIT labels its B.Sc. Fashion Design as a three-year programme.

Programme structures can differ under institutional implementation of undergraduate curriculum frameworks. For example, Guru Nanak Dev University lists three-year B.Sc. Fashion Designing as well as four-year B.Sc. (Honours) and B.Sc. (Honours with Research) Fashion Designing options. Confirm the exact exit award, credit structure and availability of an honours year with the institution.
bullets: Common route: 3 years / 6 semesters.
Possible alternative at some universities: 4-year honours or honours-with-research structure.
The programme title may appear as Fashion Design, Fashion Designing, or Fashion & Apparel Design.

sections.heading: What students study
paragraphs: Course content is institution-specific, but the degree generally integrates creative design practice with textile and apparel-production knowledge. VIT’s published B.Sc. Fashion Design content includes textile science, wet processing and garment finishing, fashion illustration, patternmaking, computer-aided designing, surface ornamentation, fashion history, basics of design, and portfolio work.

Practical learning is a material part of the programme model rather than an optional add-on: VIT describes hands-on textile and design exploration, portfolio development, practical classes and industrial exposure. Students should inspect the semester-wise syllabus to check the balance of studio work, garment construction, software/CAD, manufacturing, merchandising and internships.
bullets: Design and visual communication: fashion illustration, design fundamentals, fashion history and portfolio development.
Technical and production subjects: textile science, wet processing, garment finishing and patternmaking.
Digital and surface practice: computer-aided design and surface ornamentation.
Professional exposure may include practical classes, industry exposure and portfolio-building, depending on the provider.

sections.heading: Admission routes: institution-specific, not automatically through NIFT
paragraphs: Admission procedures are set by the university or college. CSMU’s ordinance permits selection through qualifying-exam merit, an admission test and/or personal interview, subject to the university’s rules for that session. VIT’s published B.Sc. Fashion Design procedure instead states that there is no entrance examination; its ranking uses qualifying-exam marks and a personal interview.

The NIFT Entrance Examination should not be treated as a universal prerequisite for this node. NIFT’s own undergraduate page identifies Fashion Design as a B.Des. programme at NIFT. Therefore, use NIFT’s admission prospectus only when applying specifically to NIFT’s B.Des. Fashion Design; follow each B.Sc. provider’s separate application and selection instructions.
bullets: Check whether the provider uses Class 12 merit, a design/entrance test, a portfolio review, an interview, or a combination.
Check the admission-year notice for application dates, required documents, category rules and counselling/seat-allotment steps.
Do not infer that admission to a non-NIFT B.Sc. Fashion Design requires the NIFT entrance exam.

sections.heading: Costs: compare current institutional fee notices
paragraphs: There is no standard national fee for B.Sc. Fashion Design. Published amounts vary substantially by institution and may exclude or separately list examination, hostel, uniform, materials, registration, refundable deposits and other charges.

As dated fee context rather than a current nationwide benchmark, VIT’s 2024–25 B.Sc. Fashion Design page lists ₹90,000 tuition and a one-time refundable ₹2,000 caution deposit. DBUU’s fee page published for 2026 lists an All India first-semester academic fee of ₹40,000 before listed scholarships, plus separate one-time and recurring charges. These examples are not interchangeable quotations or guarantees; applicants should request the fee schedule and refund policy for the exact academic session.
bullets: Ask for a written fee breakup covering tuition, admission/registration, examinations, studio or laboratory charges, uniform/materials, hostel and refundable deposits.
Check whether scholarships are automatic, merit-based, domicile-based or conditional on payment dates.
Treat published fees as date- and campus-specific.

sections.heading: After the degree: fashion roles, entrepreneurship and further study
paragraphs: The degree can support entry-level work across design and apparel-related functions, with the actual role depending on a graduate’s portfolio, technical ability, internships and employer requirements. CSMU identifies potential paths including fashion designer and merchandiser, alongside work connected with apparel, e-commerce, retail, branding and marketing. VIT also describes the programme as preparing graduates for fashion-design work and higher studies.

Further study is a possible route for specialisation. For example, Guru Nanak Dev University lists M.Sc. Fashion Designing among the offerings of its apparel and textile technology department. Eligibility for postgraduate study is programme-specific, so graduates should check subject, marks and entrance requirements directly with the target university.

Fashion-label entrepreneurship is also a plausible progression, but a degree alone does not establish a label. CSMU frames entrepreneurship development, consumer behaviour and digital technologies as programme outcomes; prospective founders should additionally plan for portfolio development, production/sourcing capability, finance, branding, compliance and market validation.
bullets: Design-facing paths: fashion designer, stylist, costume-related work and portfolio-led creative roles.
Commercial and operations paths: fashion merchandising, apparel/retail functions, branding, e-commerce, sourcing and quality or production roles where the employer’s criteria are met.
Education pathway: relevant master’s programmes such as Fashion Designing/Fashion Technology/Textiles, subject to each university’s eligibility rules.
Entrepreneurship pathway: develop a distinct product proposition, portfolio, supplier/manufacturing network and commercial plan before launching a label.
